title: Gaussian Shell Maps for Efficient 3D Human Generation
abstract: Efficient generation of 3D digital humans is important in several industries, including virtual reality, social media, and cinematic production. 3D generative adversarial networks (GANs) have demonstrated state-of-the-art (SOTA) quality and diversity for generated assets. Current 3D GAN architectures, however, typically rely on volume representations, which are slow to render, thereby hampering the GAN training and requiring multi-view-inconsistent 2D upsamplers. Here, we introduce Gaussian Shell Maps (GSMs) as a framework that connects SOTA generator network architectures with emerging 3D Gaussian rendering primitives using an articulable multi shell-based scaffold. In this setting, a CNN generates a 3D texture stack with features that are mapped to the shells. The latter represent inflated and deflated versions of a template surface of a digital human in a canonical body pose. Instead of rasterizing the shells directly, we sample 3D Gaussians on the shells whose attributes are encoded in the texture features. These Gaussians are efficiently and differentiably rendered. The ability to articulate the shells is important during GAN training and, at inference time, to deform a body i

sections.title: Gaussian Shell Maps for Efficient 3D Human Generation
sections.summary: Rameen Abdal *1 Wang Yifan *1 Zifan Shi *†1,2 Yinghao Xu 1 Ryan Po 1 Zhengfei Kuang 1 Qifeng Chen 2 Dit-Yan Yeung 2 Gordon Wetzstein 1 1 Stanford University, USA 2 HKUST, Hong Kong Figure 1. Gaussian Shell Maps.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.summary: In this setting, a CNN generates a 3D texture stack with features that are mapped to the shells. Instead of rasterizing the shells directly, we sample 3D Gaussians on the shells whose attributes are encoded in the texture features.

sections.title: 1. Introduction
sections.summary: Inspired by the traditional computer graphics work on shell maps [47], GSMs use the CNN generator to produce texture maps for a set of 'shell' meshes appropriately inflated and deflated from the popular SMPL mesh template for human bodies [37]. The textures on the individual shells directly encode the properties of 3D Gaussians, which are sampled on the shell surfaces at fixed locations.

sections.title: 2. Related Work
sections.summary: 3), we sample 3D Gaussians on 'shells', which are mesh layers offsetted from the SMPL template, forming a shell volume to model complex and diverse geometry and appearance; the Gaussian parameters are learned in the texture space, allowing us to leverage existing CNN-based generative architecture (Sec. While the Gaussian primitives have been used for efficient scene reconstruction and novel view synthesis, it is not trivial to deploy Gaussians in a generative setup.

sections.title: 3. Background
sections.summary: <!-- image --> maps such that every point in the shell volume can be bijectively mapped to the 3D texture map for efficient modeling and rendering in texture space. Formally, the mapping from x in the shell volume to its position in the 3D texture map is defined as <!-- formula-not-decoded --> where T , T t refer to corresponding tetrahedra in the shell and texture space, B ( x , T ) is the barycentric coordinates of x in T , and ϕ denotes barycentric interpolation.

sections.title: 4. Gaussian Shell Maps (GSM)
sections.summary: GSM is a framework that connects 3D Gaussians with SOTA CNN-based generator networks [11] in a GAN setting. The key idea is to anchor the 3D Gaussians at fixed locations on a set of 'shells' derived from the SMPL [37] human body template mesh.

sections.title: 4.1. Representation
sections.summary: Our method utilizes the concept of shell maps to leverage the inherent planar structure of texture space for seamless integration with CNN-based generative architectures and, at the same time, encapsulate diverse and complex surface details without directly modifying the template mesh. Once sampled, the Gaussians are anchored on the shells using barycentric coordinates so that every Gaussian center x can be mapped to a point x t on the corresponding shell map using Eq.

sections.title: 4.2. Deformation
sections.summary: Note that, different from SMPL, our template mesh could be any of the shells. In particular, given the barycentric coordinates B ( x , T ) of a Gaussian inside triangle T , and the deformed position T new and the rotation quaternions P on the vertices, we can obtain the new location and orientation of the Gaussian as <!-- formula-not-decoded --> <!-- formula-not-decoded --> The deformed mesh is given by the SMPL deformation model SMPL , and the quaternions P are a result of the linear blend skinning (LBS) from regressed joints and skinning weights, { w j } N J 1 , i.e., M new = SMPL( θ , β , M ) and p = rot2quat ( ∑ N J j =1 w j R j ( θ , J ( β )) ) , where R j is the rotation matrix of the j -th joint, and J is the regressor function that maps the shape parameters to the joint locations.

sections.title: 4.3. GAN training
sections.summary: Since it does not have an upsampler, the input to the discriminator is the RGB image concatenated with the alpha channel (foreground mask), which is rendered using Gaussian rasterization with the Gaussian color set to 1 for fake samples and precomputed using off-the-shelf segmentation network [55] for the real samples. We compare our method with 3D-GAN baselines using DeepFashion and SHHQ datasets.

sections.title: 5. Experiment Settings
sections.summary: Không có nội dung để tóm tắt.

sections.title: 5.1. Datasets
sections.summary: Weevaluate our method using the two most common human datasets, DeepFashion [36] and SHHQ [17]. DeepFashion and SHHQ do not provide SMPL parameters, so we use Table 2.

sections.title: 5.2. Training Details
sections.summary: By default, we generate the shell maps at 512 × 512 resolution for 8 shells with a total of 100 k Gaussians equally distributed across the shells. R 1 regularization is used for all the discriminators with a weight of 10 .

sections.title: 5.3. Evaluation Details
sections.summary: We compare with the following volume rendering and rasterization-based methods: EG3D [11], StyleSDF [42], GNARF [6], ENARF [41], StylePeople [19], EVA3D [23], GetAvatar [76], and AG3D [15]. <!-- image --> AG3D Ours Figure 6.

sections.title: 6. Experiment Results
sections.summary: Không có nội dung để tóm tắt.

sections.title: 6.1. Qualitative
sections.summary: Figure 1 showcases some of our generated results. Our method is able to model the complex geometry and appearance of the human body, generating characters with loose clothing and accessories (see Figure 1) and producing convincing results under various articulations.

sections.title: 6.2. Quantitative
sections.summary: In Table 1, we show the results of our method compared to the competing methods. Many of the methods have not released training or data processing code; we have collected these numbers with our best effort from the authors directly.

sections.title: 6.3. Ablation
sections.summary: The number of shells determines the granularity of variation we can model inside the shell volume, thus impacting the expressivity of our representation. Mask and Part Discriminator The mask discriminator involves the inclusion of the alpha channel as part of the input to the RGB images and is very beneficial for disambiguating background by learning plausible human silhouettes.

sections.title: 7. Discussion
sections.summary: Our method is not without limitations. We thank the authors of 3D Gaussian Splatting, Bernhard Kerbl, Georgios Kopanas, and George Drettakis for the discussions on the code.
